name: Relations and Functions
subject: Maths

sections.heading: 1.1 Relations — Setup
sections.body:
A relation $R$ from set $A$ to set $B$ is any subset of the Cartesian product $A\times B$. If $(a,b)\in R$ we write $aRb$. A relation in $A$ means a subset of $A\times A$. Two extremes: the empty relation $R=\varnothing$ (no element related to any) and the universal relation $R=A\times A$ (every element related to every).

Domain of $R$ = set of first coordinates appearing; range = set of second coordinates; codomain = $B$ (range $\subseteq$ codomain).

sections.heading: 1.2 Types of Relations
sections.body:
A relation $R$ in $A$ is:

  • Reflexive if $(a,a)\in R\;\forall a\in A$.
  • Symmetric if $(a_1,a_2)\in R\Rightarrow(a_2,a_1)\in R$.
  • Transitive if $(a_1,a_2),(a_2,a_3)\in R\Rightarrow(a_1,a_3)\in R$.
  • Equivalence relation if reflexive, symmetric AND transitive.

An equivalence relation partitions $A$ into equivalence classes $[a]=\{x\in A:xRa\}$. Distinct classes are disjoint and their union is $A$.

Example. $R=\{(a,b)\in\mathbb Z\times\mathbb Z:\,a-b\text{ is divisible by }n\}$ is an equivalence relation; classes are residue classes mod $n$.

sections.heading: 1.3 Functions — Types
sections.body:
A function $f:A\to B$ is a relation in which every element of $A$ has exactly one image in $B$.

  • One-one (injective): $f(x_1)=f(x_2)\Rightarrow x_1=x_2$.
  • Onto (surjective): range $=$ codomain, i.e. $\forall y\in B\;\exists x\in A$ with $f(x)=y$.
  • Bijective: one-one AND onto.
  • Many-one: not injective. Into: not surjective.

Useful theorem: if $A$ and $B$ are finite with the same cardinality and $f:A\to B$, then $f$ is one-one $\Leftrightarrow$ $f$ is onto.

sections.heading: 1.4 Composition of Functions
sections.body:
If $f:A\to B$ and $g:B\to C$, the composition $g\circ f:A\to C$ is defined by $(g\circ f)(x)=g(f(x))$.

Properties:

  • Composition is associative: $h\circ(g\circ f)=(h\circ g)\circ f$.
  • Composition is not commutative in general: $g\circ f\neq f\circ g$.
  • If $f,g$ are one-one then $g\circ f$ is one-one.
  • If $f,g$ are onto then $g\circ f$ is onto.
keyFormulae: $g\circ f$ defined only if range$(f)\subseteq$ domain$(g)$

sections.heading: 1.5 Invertible Functions
sections.body:
$f:A\to B$ is invertible if there exists $g:B\to A$ such that $g\circ f=I_A$ and $f\circ g=I_B$. Then $g=f^{-1}$.

Theorem. $f$ is invertible $\Leftrightarrow$ $f$ is bijective. The inverse is unique.

If $f:A\to B$ and $g:B\to C$ are bijective then $(g\circ f)^{-1}=f^{-1}\circ g^{-1}$ (sock-shoe rule).

sections.heading: 1.6 Binary Operations
sections.body:
A binary operation $*$ on a set $A$ is a function $*:A\times A\to A$. We write $a*b$ instead of $*(a,b)$.

  • Commutative: $a*b=b*a\;\forall a,b\in A$.
  • Associative: $(a*b)*c=a*(b*c)$.
  • Identity $e\in A$: $a*e=e*a=a\;\forall a$. (Unique if it exists.)
  • Inverse of $a$: element $b$ such that $a*b=b*a=e$. (Unique if $*$ is associative.)

Examples: $+,\times$ on $\mathbb R$ are commutative and associative; subtraction is neither commutative nor associative; matrix multiplication is associative but not commutative.

summary: Relations are subsets of $A\times B$; classify by reflexive/symmetric/transitive; equivalence relations partition the set into classes. Functions are special relations; classify as one-one, onto, bijective. Composition is associative not commutative; a function is invertible iff bijective with $(g\circ f)^{-1}=f^{-1}\circ g^{-1}$. Binary operations may have identity and inverses.
pitfalls: Confusing codomain with range — codomain is given, range is the actual image.
Assuming reflexive automatically holds — must verify $(a,a)\in R$ for EVERY $a$.
Writing $(g\circ f)(x)=f(g(x))$ — the rightmost function acts first.
Claiming $f$ is invertible without checking BOTH injectivity and surjectivity.
Forgetting that a binary operation must be closed — output must lie in $A$.
